title: Add `qbfsat` command to integrate exists-forall solving and specialization
body:
This PR adds a new command, `qbfsat`, to solve combinational exists-forall problems.  These problems are useful for a number of different applications, including syntax-guided synthesis (SyGuS), circuit [debugging](https://www.epfl.ch/labs/lap/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/BeckerNov15_FudgeFactorSyntaxGuidedSynthesisForAccurateRtlErrorLocalizationAndCorrection_HVC15.pdf), [GLIFT optimization](https://www.epfl.ch/labs/lap/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/BeckerJun17_ArbitraryPrecisionAndComplexityTradeoffsForGateLevelInformationFlowTracking_DAC17.pdf), and [solver-aided design optimization](https://infoscience.epfl.ch/record/257026).

Exactly one module is expected to be selected, just like the `sat` command.

Existentially-quantified variables can be created with the `$anyconst` cell.  Regular `$allconst` cells can be used to declare universally-quantified variables, but module inputs will automatically be converted into `$allconst`-driven wires.

Regular `$assume` cells work fine, but the `-assume-outputs` option will automatically conjoin any 1-bit module outputs and `$assume` that conjunction, which is useful if your module describes a QBF-SAT miter.

This PR also supports post-solution specialization, where the model from a successfully solved QBF-SAT instance is imported back into the module and the values found by the solver for the existentially-quantified variables are assigned to the appropriate `$anyconst`-receiving wire.  Solutions (i.e. models) can also be written to a file, and modules can be specialized from those solution files.  This specialization enables seamless circuit sketching, as described in [this paper](https://www.epfl.ch/labs/lap/wp-content/uploads/2018/05/BeckerMar14_SketchilogSketchingCombinationalCircuits_DATE14.pdf).

The solution specialization also allows different modules containing signals with the same names as the solved module to be specialized, as well, which is useful for e.g. evaluating a sub-circuit of the miter.
